title: Change in charge density wave order beyond the Lifshitz transition in 2H-Ta\textsubscript{1$\pmδ$}S\textsubscript{2}
authors: Mihir Date, Jan Berges, Enrico Da Como, Marcin Mucha-Kruczyński, Alex Louat, Gabriele Domaine, Niels B. M. Schröter, Malte Rösner, Matthew D. Watson
abstract: We investigate electronic instabilities in 2H-TaS\textsubscript{2} and a self-intercalated variant, 2H$^\dagger$-Ta\textsubscript{1+$δ$}S\textsubscript{2}. In conventional samples, which we determine to be slightly hole-doped, spectral gaps and backfolded features are found as fingerprints of the $3\times3$ charge density wave (CDW). Notably, the backfolded features emerge only at a temperatures below $T\approx$~65~K, substantially lower than the established CDW temperature of 78~K, suggesting an incommensurate-commensurate lock-in transition analogous to the phenomenology of the 2H-TaSe\textsubscript{2}. In contrast, the self-intercalated 2H$^\dagger$ sample exhibits substantial electron doping and signatures of a novel \tworootthree CDW. Using \textit{ab initio} calculations of the phonon spectrum, we demonstrate that the \threebythree instability ($\mathbf{q}=\sfrac{2}{3}\mathbf{ΓM}$) is highly sensitive to band filling. Furthermore, with increased interlayer spacing, a competing soft phonon mode emerges near $\mathbf{q}=\sfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{ΓK}$, corresponding to the superstructure observed in the 2H$^\dagger$ phase, although in our calculations this instability arises under hole doping rather than the electron doping inferred experimentally. These results establish band filling and interlayer spacing as key control parameters for CDW ordering vectors in 2H-TaS\textsubscript{2}, and highlight a route to engineering electronic instabilities in a prototypical layered material.
summary: This paper investigates how tuning the electron density and lattice structure in TaS2 can engineer different charge density wave phases. It reveals a hidden lock-in transition in standard TaS2 and identifies a new, novel CDW order in self-intercalated samples. This work provides a roadmap for controlling electronic instabilities in layered materials via chemical doping.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the change in charge density wave (CDW) ordering in 2H-TaS2 and its self-intercalated variant, specifically investigating how band filling and interlayer spacing control the CDW wavevectors and the nature of the incommensurate-to-commensurate transition.
main_result: The authors identify a two-step transition in conventional 2H-TaS2 involving a lock-in transition below 65 K and discover a novel 2√3 x 2√3R(30°) CDW phase in electron-doped 2H†-Ta1+δS2.
method: The study combines micro-focused angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (μ-ARPES) with ab initio calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and the Electron-Phonon Wannier (EPW) package to model phonon instabilities and band filling effects.
model_or_system: The physical system is the transition metal dichalcogenide 2H-TaS2 and its self-intercalated version 2H†-Ta1+δS2, characterized by quasi-2D layers and electronic instabilities driven by electron-phonon coupling.
key_observables: Spectral gaps, backfolded bands (triangular pockets), Fermi surface topology (dogbone and K-barrel pockets), and phonon dispersion/self-energy.
important_parameters: Temperature (25 K to 100 K), band filling (electron vs. hole doping), and interlayer spacing (c-axis elongation).
main_assumptions: The calculations assume the validity of the electron-phonon coupling model as implemented in the EPW package and use fixed Ta positions with relaxed S z-coordinates.
figures_summary: The figures likely illustrate ARPES spectra showing spectral gaps and backfolded features, Fermi surface reconstructions, and theoretical phonon dispersion curves showing instabilities at specific q-vectors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of CDW ordering, presents experimental ARPES evidence for the lock-in transition in conventional TaS2, describes the discovery of the new CDW phase in the self-intercalated phase, and concludes with ab initio theoretical support for the role of doping and lattice spacing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fermi surface topology in 2H-TaS2 from experiment and DFT calculations. (a) Crystal structure of 2Ha-TaS2. (b) Comparison of measured Fermi surfaces at 100 K and 25 K, above and well below the 3×3 transition temperature, with the normal (black) and reconstructed (orange) Brillouin zones overlaid (hν = 58 eV, integrating spectral weight within 5 meV of EF ). Fermi surfaces calculated at (c) EF , and (d) for a constant energy 50 meV below the Fermi energy. The latter shows the dogbone topology as in the experiments. Scale bars represent 0.5 Å−1.

Fig. 1(a) shows the crystal structure of 2H-TaS2 consisting of two trigonal prismatically stacked layers which are alter- nately rotated by 180◦, with the Ta atoms directly on top of each other, following the 2Ha stacking [27]. The Fermi sur- face measured in the high temperature undistorted phase at 102 K (left half of Fig. 1(b)) contains 3 closed quasi-2D Fermi surfaces: a central “circular" pocket around Γ, twofold sym- metric “dogbone” pockets centered at M, and a single closed “barrel” around the K-points. Contrastingly, at a temperature of 25 K, the K-barrel is completely gapped, and the dogbone pockets develop partial spectral gaps, due to the formation of the 3×3 CDW [20]. This...

FIG. 2. Temperature-dependent ARPES on 2H-TaS2. (a)-(f) Fermi surfaces of 2H-TaS2 measured at hν = 58 eV in the temperature range of 25 - 100 K, showing the incommensurate CDW transition at approximately 78 K (TICDW), and the lock-in transition around 55 K (TCCDW). (g)-(i) Dispersion along the direction marked by the dashed orange line in (a), at 25, 65, and 100 K respectively. (j) EDCs at a constant momentum. (k,l,m) Corresponding MDCs near EF are displayed in (k), (l), and (m). There is no discernible difference between the 65 K and 100 K data.

FIG. 3. Phonon bandstructures for regularly stacked 2H-TaS2 with an interlayer spacing of 3.01 Å. (a) With changing doping lev- els, the longitudinal acoustic phonon mode softens at different points along Γ −M. (b) Phonon dispersion zoomed in the Γ −M direc- tion. The LA phonons in this region soften between 1/2ΓM and M as the chemical potential is tuned from electron to hole doping.
